headline_summary: Iran says it struck an Israel-linked vessel in/near the Strait of Hormuz as maritime incidents persist at the key oil chokepoint.
Iran signals conditional/managed transit in the Strait of Hormuz for certain vessels, implying continued disruption risk rather than full normalization.
U.S. and partners weigh additional force-protection and air-defense measures in the Gulf after drone/missile incidents near energy infrastructure.
Diplomatic activity continues among Gulf states, the U.S., and European partners amid competing proposals on reopening/operating rules for Hormuz transits.
Market focus remains on oil supply risk premium, EM FX pressure from energy import costs, and broader risk-off flows tied to escalation headlines.
risk_drivers: Strait of Hormuz: intermittent/conditional transit and continued security incidents
Attack risk to tankers/LNG carriers and knock-on insurance/freight constraints
Gulf energy infrastructure vulnerability (refineries, ports, power)
Escalation ladder dynamics (ultimatums/retaliation cycles) creating headline-driven volatility
Policy uncertainty around sanctions, maritime rules, and military protection missions

Energy: oil_up_risk: persistent geopolitical premium while Hormuz remains insecure/managed
Higher freight/war-risk insurance can tighten effective supply and raise delivered costs
Refined product cracks risk if Gulf facilities face intermittent outages
FX: em_fx_pressure: energy-importer EM currencies face terms-of-trade stress on oil spikes
risk_off_flows: USD/JPY/CHF bid during escalation headlines; pressure on high-beta FX
Rates: rates_credit_spreads: wider EM/HY spreads on risk-off; inflation breakevens sensitive to crude
Duration can whipsaw if growth fears rise while oil lifts inflation expectations
Equities: risk_off_flows: airlines/transport and consumer cyclicals vulnerable to fuel and risk-off shocks
Relative support for energy/defense; higher volatility in GCC markets

sources.title: Iran says it hit Israel-linked vessel in Hormuz Strait
Exclusive: US intelligence warns Iran unlikely to ease Hormuz Strait chokehold soon, sources say
World anxious to open Hormuz Strait while Trump and Iran trade threats
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up

events.title: Iran says it struck an Israel-linked vessel in/near the Strait of Hormuz
events.implication: Signals elevated maritime attack risk at the world’s key oil chokepoint; can further raise war-risk premia, insurance, and routing delays.

events.title: Iran signals conditional passage rules for some vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz
events.implication: Managed/conditional transit implies continued disruption risk; even partial easing can be reversed quickly and keeps freight and price volatility elevated.

events.title: Pakistan-mediated two-stage ceasefire framework ('Islamabad Accord') circulated to Iran and the United States
events.implication: Ceasefire proposal explicitly tied to reopening Hormuz; acceptance/rejection is a key driver of near-term energy risk premium.

events.title: Iranian media report Tehran rejected a U.S. 48-hour ceasefire proposal
events.implication: Ceasefire rejection implies higher probability of continued strikes and maritime disruption; supports risk-off and oil-upside skew.

events.title: Israel preparing potential attacks on Iranian energy sites pending U.S. green light, official says
events.implication: Threat to Iranian energy infrastructure raises tail risk of supply disruptions and sharp oil spikes; increases market volatility.

events.title: Japanese-owned LNG tanker crosses Strait of Hormuz amid conflict-driven disruption risk
events.implication: Demonstrates partial continuity of flows but at elevated security/insurance cost; LNG supply chain remains vulnerable to interruption.

events.title: Oil prices surge anew as hopes dim for swift end to Iran war, Reuters reports
events.implication: Energy price reaction embeds a growing geopolitical risk premium; feeds inflation expectations and tightens financial conditions.

events.title: Industry analysis warns securing Strait of Hormuz would be harder than Red Sea efforts
events.implication: Suggests prolonged disruption potential if conflict persists; supports higher structural freight/insurance costs and oil risk premium.

events.title: Dubai crude benchmark comes under stress as Iran war pressures Middle East pricing, Reuters says
events.implication: Regional benchmark dislocations can transmit to global crude differentials and refinery margins; increases hedging demand and volatility.

events.title: Iran conflict disrupts global shipping as tankers are stranded or damaged, Reuters reports
events.location_name: Strait of Hormuz, near United Arab Emirates
events.implication: Shipping disruptions and vessel damage amplify effective supply shocks and raise delivered energy costs; negative for global trade and risk assets.

events.title: Maritime advisory warns Red Sea corridor risks could resurface amid wider regional escalation
events.implication: If Red Sea risk re-intensifies alongside Hormuz, global shipping detours would widen inflationary pressures and deepen risk-off moves.

events.title: BBC analysis flags potential Houthi threat to Red Sea shipping as global economic risk
events.implication: Renewed Red Sea attacks would compound Gulf chokepoint risks and raise shipping/energy costs; bearish for global growth-sensitive assets.

events.title: Last-ditch 45-day ceasefire talks reported among U.S., Iran and regional mediators
events.implication: A temporary ceasefire could reduce immediate shipping risk premia, but negotiation uncertainty sustains volatility until terms are clear.

events.title: U.S.-Egypt-Qatar joint statement reiterates mediation framework (context for Gaza and regional diplomacy)
events.implication: Diplomacy in Gaza can interact with broader regional escalation dynamics and risk sentiment, though direct market impact is second-order.

events.title: Reports say Gaza ceasefire deal is 'very close' with potential 48-hour window
events.implication: Any Gaza de-escalation could marginally reduce regional risk premia, but is likely overshadowed by Iran/Hormuz dynamics in markets.

links.from: Pakistan-mediated two-stage ceasefire framework ('Islamabad Accord') circulated to Iran and the United States
links.to: Iran signals conditional passage rules for some vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz
links.why: Reuters reports ceasefire framework explicitly envisages reopening Hormuz; acceptance could loosen transit restrictions.

links.from: Iranian media report Tehran rejected a U.S. 48-hour ceasefire proposal
links.to: Israel preparing potential attacks on Iranian energy sites pending U.S. green light, official says
links.why: Rejection of ceasefire increases probability of continued/intensified strikes, including against energy sites.

links.from: Iran says it struck an Israel-linked vessel in/near the Strait of Hormuz
links.why: Attack risk at Hormuz can directly threaten oil/LNG flows and raise war-risk/insurance costs.

links.from: Iran signals conditional passage rules for some vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz
links.why: Uncertain transit rules create recurring escalation headlines, supporting safe-haven flows.

links.from: Israel preparing potential attacks on Iranian energy sites pending U.S. green light, official says
links.why: Potential strikes on energy infrastructure increase probability of material supply disruption beyond shipping effects.

links.from: Oil prices surge anew as hopes dim for swift end to Iran war, Reuters reports
links.why: Higher oil prices typically pressure oil-importer EM FX via higher import bills and inflation.

links.from: Industry analysis warns securing Strait of Hormuz would be harder than Red Sea efforts
links.why: Prolonged chokepoint insecurity can worsen inflation/growth trade-offs and widen risk premia in credit and EM.

links.from: Maritime advisory warns Red Sea corridor risks could resurface amid wider regional escalation
links.why: Simultaneous Red Sea and Hormuz risks would tighten effective supply and lift energy/shipping costs.
